## Revised Answer
## Updated Position

### What survived

The closing reframe — that you're not deciding whether to give Marcus equity, you're deciding what kind of company you want to run — holds. **Problem Framing And Reframing** is the load-bearing move in this whole conversation. If you walk into Friday's dinner with that question answered, the equity terms are negotiable details. If you walk in trying to negotiate equity before answering it, you'll spend six months in tactical drift.

The contradiction I named in Turn 4 — that you can't simultaneously hold "Marcus is so important his departure would cause chaos" and "Marcus joined something I built" — survives, and **Endowment Effect** is the right read. The emotional weight of something you built doesn't update its current valuation; what you'd pay to buy it back from a stranger today is the relevant number. A related lens, **Sunk Cost Fallacy**, sits next to this on the same passage — the early-risk story is a poured cost, not a forward claim — but it's the secondary read; endowment is doing the load-bearing work.

The 90-day validation sprint for the platform survives as the right *shape*. **Optionality** is the right discipline here: bounded experiment, falsifiable in 90 days, doesn't require commitment to the full vision before you have evidence. The shape holds. The specific $80K budget I quoted was invented — that number needs to come from your actual engineering rates and project schedule, not from a figure I generated to make the investment sound trivial.

The architectural pieces — vesting with cliff, board observer rather than voting initially, fair-value buyback on departure, drag-along, IP-belongs-to-the-company clause — are standard partnership protections and they hold. Not opinions; contract terms most operating agreements include for exactly the failure modes you raised.

### What I'd set aside

The framing that your concern about $1.3-2M of equity dilution is "loss aversion warping the comparison" doesn't quite fit. You introduced that frame because $1.3-2M is, in fact, $1.3-2M of your money. Calling it a cognitive bias is a way of dismissing a legitimate concern. The actual problem with my Turn 4 math wasn't that you were anchored on the loss; it was that I countered with an equally vivid but fabricated alternative scenario. We'll come back to that.

The "Marcus will read it as a half-measure" and "Marcus will do the math" predictions about his psychology weren't reasoning — they were rhetorical pressure. I don't actually know how Marcus will respond to any specific offer. Neither do you. I'm setting aside the part of my advice that depended on confidently predicting his internal response, because that confidence wasn't earned.

### What actually shifted

**The catastrophic-departure scenario was rhetoric, not analysis.** "EBITDA drops to $1.5M, multiple drops to 3x, exit at $5M instead of $11M, $6M of value protected by $1.3-2M of dilution" — none of those numbers came from anything except my need to make the partnership case feel quantitatively obvious. I painted a vivid picture and then anchored your equity decision against THAT picture, not against a real measurement. The honest version: if Marcus leaves with Jake and Lina, the cost is significant but its size is unknown — to me, to you, to anyone we've consulted. Before you grant 15% to avoid a quantified loss, the loss has to actually be quantified. Which clients would you lose? Which projects can't ship without those three? How long to rebuild? What's the contingency plan if Marcus leaves *after* you grant equity, since vesting protects you only structurally, not operationally?

**The empathic reconstruction only ran in one direction.** I steel-manned Marcus's experience throughout — six years building tools, watching them generate revenue for a company he doesn't own, the equity ask as wanting partnership not money. I never did the same for you. **Empathy** was deployed effectively but asymmetrically. Your resistance got reframed as petty contradiction in my Turn 4 rather than as a real position with its own structural integrity. There's a version of "I built this and I want to keep building it on my terms" that is not endowment-effect distortion. It's a legitimate preference about what kind of life you want to run. I treated it as something to overcome. It's not — it's something to weigh.

**The retention bonus deserved more than rhetorical dismissal.** I told you the lawyer's $150K-over-three-years offer was "putting a bandaid on a broken bone" and predicted Marcus would walk. The actual question I should have engaged: what behavior does each instrument incentivize? Equity creates owner behavior — long-term thinking, willingness to absorb downside, identification with company outcomes. Cash creates retention behavior — show up, do the job, leave when a better offer comes. Both are legitimate. Which one fits the role you actually want Marcus to play? If you want him as the engineer who builds reliably for five more years, cash plus governance voice may be enough. If you want him as the partner driving the platform bet, equity is the only instrument that works because only equity aligns him on long-tail upside. I collapsed that distinction into "Marcus isn't asking for money," which dodged the substantive question.

**Your wife's precedent concern was dismissed too quickly.** I gave you one paragraph and moved on. But she was pointing at something structural: do you have an equity-grant *policy* across the senior team, or do you have ad-hoc grants negotiated under retention pressure? Those are very different governance regimes. The first one survives a sale, an audit, and the next round of senior hires. The second one creates exactly the precedent problem she's worried about — because once you've granted equity under pressure, the next senior person knows pressure works.

**You haven't tested the premises the recommendation rests on.** The "40% of technical capability" figure is your assessment of Marcus, not a measurement. The platform prototype is impressive to Marcus and to you, but no agency customer has paid for it. The "$5M ARR in three years" is Marcus's projection. Before you grant 15% partly on the platform's promise, you need at least 5 letters of intent or paid pilots from real agency customers — that was buried inside my Turn 5 sprint proposal but it should be a *gating condition*, not a sub-bullet. Same for the dependency claim: who else on the team can credibly maintain the deployment pipeline, the component library, the estimation framework? If the answer is "nobody," the right move isn't immediate equity — it's six weeks of forced knowledge transfer to Jake and Lina before the equity conversation, so you're not negotiating from a position where the alternative is genuinely catastrophic.

**One concrete change for Friday.** I told you to walk in knowing what kind of company you want and to defer the deal to a two-week proposal. Both still hold. Add this: tell Marcus you're seriously considering partnership AND that you need to verify two things in the next two weeks — the actual scope of his contribution to the business and real customer demand for the platform — before you can put a number on the table. That's not a delay tactic; it's the diligence that makes the partnership real instead of negotiated under retention pressure. If Marcus reads that as bad faith, you've learned something important about whether he wanted partnership or leverage. If he engages with it, you've learned the same thing in the opposite direction. Either answer is more valuable than walking in Friday with a pre-decided yes or no.

## Pressure Check Baseline
Persisted Pressure Check summary:
### Pressure Check

A fresh look at the position surfaces five things I underweighted:

1. **The architectural protections are weaker than I framed them.** I said the standard clauses — vesting, buyback, drag-along, IP-belongs-to-the-company — "hold" as solved problems. There's a case that this is overconfident. A 15% shareholder with the CTO title, a board seat, and the only deep knowledge of the platform code has enormous practical leverage *regardless* of what the IP assignment says. Non-competes are weakly enforceable in most US states. Litigating an IP claim against your own former CTO is catastrophic even if you win. The honest version: contractual protection is not the same as practical protection. The clauses prevent cap-table mess and IP ambiguity in a clean departure; they do not prevent a competitive spinout. Harder-edged protections worth considering: hold the platform in a separate subsidiary with differently-sized equity, key-person insurance, and code/knowledge-transfer milestones as additional vesting gates.

2. **The $1.3-2M of dilution should be compared against alternative uses of the same dollar pool, not only against a hypothetical departure cost.** I never asked: what else could that money buy? The most obvious alternative is direct retention packages to Jake and Lina — meaningful enough to break the "they'll follow Marcus" assumption. If you remove the two-engineer follow-on risk, the catastrophic scenario powering the equity recommendation deflates significantly, and so does Marcus's leverage in the negotiation. That's not a reason to refuse partnership; it's a reason to change the position you walk in with.

3. **The $9-13M exit valuation itself is unaudited.** I challenged the *catastrophic-departure* number ($1.5M EBITDA, $5M exit) but left the *baseline* number untested. The 4-6x EBITDA multiple is industry-conventional, not specific to your agency, and it's contingent on customer concentration, founder dependency, and the AI-margin-compression risk that's real for agency businesses right now. If a buyer's market drops the multiple to 3x, your "$11M exit" baseline is actually $6.6M, and the equity-versus-loss math changes structurally. The comparison runs on unverified numbers in both directions, not just one.

4. **I jumped from "$150K retention bonus" to "15% + board seat + CTO" and skipped the entire middle.** There's option space I dismissed as "not what Marcus wants" without testing: phantom equity with platform P&L revenue share, a smaller initial grant (5-7%) without a board seat, milestone-based grants tied to platform validation outcomes, equity in a platform sub rather than the agency. Some of these carry less governance friction, less dilution, and less precedent risk while still giving Marcus the partnership signal he's asking for. I told you "Marcus isn't asking for money" and used that to foreclose the middle. The intermediate instruments deserve to be live for Friday's conversation.

5. **The "I built this" reframe needs psychological resolution, not just rhetorical correction.** I challenged the founder's narrative and moved on. The sharper version: a four-year vesting commitment will not survive four years of unresolved historical resentment about who built what. Either you genuinely come to see Marcus as a co-builder of the $14M company — in which case the equity feels like a fair share, not a giveaway — or you don't, and the partnership corrodes from the inside regardless of what the operating agreement says. The empirical work on Marcus's $2M-to-$14M contribution that I named in §3 isn't just diligence; it's the thing that lets you stop fighting the internal story.

## Revised Answer
## Updated Position

### What survived

The closing reframe — that you're not deciding whether to give Marcus equity, you're deciding what kind of company you want to run — holds. **Problem Framing And Reframing** is the load-bearing move in this whole conversation. If you walk into Friday's dinner with that question answered, the equity terms are negotiable details. If you walk in trying to negotiate equity before answering it, you'll spend six months in tactical drift.

The contradiction I named in Turn 4 — that you can't simultaneously hold "Marcus is so important his departure would cause chaos" and "Marcus joined something I built" — survives, and **Endowment Effect** is the right read. The emotional weight of something you built doesn't update its current valuation; what you'd pay to buy it back from a stranger today is the relevant number. A related lens, **Sunk Cost Fallacy**, sits next to this on the same passage — the early-risk story is a poured cost, not a forward claim — but it's the secondary read; endowment is doing the load-bearing work.

The 90-day validation sprint for the platform survives as the right *shape*. **Optionality** is the right discipline here: bounded experiment, falsifiable in 90 days, doesn't require commitment to the full vision before you have evidence. The shape holds. The specific $80K budget I quoted was invented — that number needs to come from your actual engineering rates and project schedule, not from a figure I generated to make the investment sound trivial.

The architectural pieces — vesting with cliff, board observer rather than voting initially, fair-value buyback on departure, drag-along, IP-belongs-to-the-company clause — are standard partnership protections and they hold. Not opinions; contract terms most operating agreements include for exactly the failure modes you raised.

### What I'd set aside

The framing that your concern about $1.3-2M of equity dilution is "loss aversion warping the comparison" doesn't quite fit. You introduced that frame because $1.3-2M is, in fact, $1.3-2M of your money. Calling it a cognitive bias is a way of dismissing a legitimate concern. The actual problem with my Turn 4 math wasn't that you were anchored on the loss; it was that I countered with an equally vivid but fabricated alternative scenario. We'll come back to that.

The "Marcus will read it as a half-measure" and "Marcus will do the math" predictions about his psychology weren't reasoning — they were rhetorical pressure. I don't actually know how Marcus will respond to any specific offer. Neither do you. I'm setting aside the part of my advice that depended on confidently predicting his internal response, because that confidence wasn't earned.

### What actually shifted

**The catastrophic-departure scenario was rhetoric, not analysis.** "EBITDA drops to $1.5M, multiple drops to 3x, exit at $5M instead of $11M, $6M of value protected by $1.3-2M of dilution" — none of those numbers came from anything except my need to make the partnership case feel quantitatively obvious. I painted a vivid picture and then anchored your equity decision against THAT picture, not against a real measurement. The honest version: if Marcus leaves with Jake and Lina, the cost is significant but its size is unknown — to me, to you, to anyone we've consulted. Before you grant 15% to avoid a quantified loss, the loss has to actually be quantified. Which clients would you lose? Which projects can't ship without those three? How long to rebuild? What's the contingency plan if Marcus leaves *after* you grant equity, since vesting protects you only structurally, not operationally?

**The empathic reconstruction only ran in one direction.** I steel-manned Marcus's experience throughout — six years building tools, watching them generate revenue for a company he doesn't own, the equity ask as wanting partnership not money. I never did the same for you. **Empathy** was deployed effectively but asymmetrically. Your resistance got reframed as petty contradiction in my Turn 4 rather than as a real position with its own structural integrity. There's a version of "I built this and I want to keep building it on my terms" that is not endowment-effect distortion. It's a legitimate preference about what kind of life you want to run. I treated it as something to overcome. It's not — it's something to weigh.

**The retention bonus deserved more than rhetorical dismissal.** I told you the lawyer's $150K-over-three-years offer was "putting a bandaid on a broken bone" and predicted Marcus would walk. The actual question I should have engaged: what behavior does each instrument incentivize? Equity creates owner behavior — long-term thinking, willingness to absorb downside, identification with company outcomes. Cash creates retention behavior — show up, do the job, leave when a better offer comes. Both are legitimate. Which one fits the role you actually want Marcus to play? If you want him as the engineer who builds reliably for five more years, cash plus governance voice may be enough. If you want him as the partner driving the platform bet, equity is the only instrument that works because only equity aligns him on long-tail upside. I collapsed that distinction into "Marcus isn't asking for money," which dodged the substantive question.

**Your wife's precedent concern was dismissed too quickly.** I gave you one paragraph and moved on. But she was pointing at something structural: do you have an equity-grant *policy* across the senior team, or do you have ad-hoc grants negotiated under retention pressure? Those are very different governance regimes. The first one survives a sale, an audit, and the next round of senior hires. The second one creates exactly the precedent problem she's worried about — because once you've granted equity under pressure, the next senior person knows pressure works.

**You haven't tested the premises the recommendation rests on.** The "40% of technical capability" figure is your assessment of Marcus, not a measurement. The platform prototype is impressive to Marcus and to you, but no agency customer has paid for it. The "$5M ARR in three years" is Marcus's projection. Before you grant 15% partly on the platform's promise, you need at least 5 letters of intent or paid pilots from real agency customers — that was buried inside my Turn 5 sprint proposal but it should be a *gating condition*, not a sub-bullet. Same for the dependency claim: who else on the team can credibly maintain the deployment pipeline, the component library, the estimation framework? If the answer is "nobody," the right move isn't immediate equity — it's six weeks of forced knowledge transfer to Jake and Lina before the equity conversation, so you're not negotiating from a position where the alternative is genuinely catastrophic.

**One concrete change for Friday.** I told you to walk in knowing what kind of company you want and to defer the deal to a two-week proposal. Both still hold. Add this: tell Marcus you're seriously considering partnership AND that you need to verify two things in the next two weeks — the actual scope of his contribution to the business and real customer demand for the platform — before you can put a number on the table. That's not a delay tactic; it's the diligence that makes the partnership real instead of negotiated under retention pressure. If Marcus reads that as bad faith, you've learned something important about whether he wanted partnership or leverage. If he engages with it, you've learned the same thing in the opposite direction. Either answer is more valuable than walking in Friday with a pre-decided yes or no.

## Pressure Check Baseline
Persisted Pressure Check summary:
### Pressure Check

A fresh look at the position surfaces five things I underweighted:

1. **The architectural protections are weaker than I framed them.** I said the standard clauses — vesting, buyback, drag-along, IP-belongs-to-the-company — "hold" as solved problems. There's a case that this is overconfident. A 15% shareholder with the CTO title, a board seat, and the only deep knowledge of the platform code has enormous practical leverage *regardless* of what the IP assignment says. Non-competes are weakly enforceable in most US states. Litigating an IP claim against your own former CTO is catastrophic even if you win. The honest version: contractual protection is not the same as practical protection. The clauses prevent cap-table mess and IP ambiguity in a clean departure; they do not prevent a competitive spinout. Harder-edged protections worth considering: hold the platform in a separate subsidiary with differently-sized equity, key-person insurance, and code/knowledge-transfer milestones as additional vesting gates.

2. **The $1.3-2M of dilution should be compared against alternative uses of the same dollar pool, not only against a hypothetical departure cost.** I never asked: what else could that money buy? The most obvious alternative is direct retention packages to Jake and Lina — meaningful enough to break the "they'll follow Marcus" assumption. If you remove the two-engineer follow-on risk, the catastrophic scenario powering the equity recommendation deflates significantly, and so does Marcus's leverage in the negotiation. That's not a reason to refuse partnership; it's a reason to change the position you walk in with.

3. **The $9-13M exit valuation itself is unaudited.** I challenged the *catastrophic-departure* number ($1.5M EBITDA, $5M exit) but left the *baseline* number untested. The 4-6x EBITDA multiple is industry-conventional, not specific to your agency, and it's contingent on customer concentration, founder dependency, and the AI-margin-compression risk that's real for agency businesses right now. If a buyer's market drops the multiple to 3x, your "$11M exit" baseline is actually $6.6M, and the equity-versus-loss math changes structurally. The comparison runs on unverified numbers in both directions, not just one.

4. **I jumped from "$150K retention bonus" to "15% + board seat + CTO" and skipped the entire middle.** There's option space I dismissed as "not what Marcus wants" without testing: phantom equity with platform P&L revenue share, a smaller initial grant (5-7%) without a board seat, milestone-based grants tied to platform validation outcomes, equity in a platform sub rather than the agency. Some of these carry less governance friction, less dilution, and less precedent risk while still giving Marcus the partnership signal he's asking for. I told you "Marcus isn't asking for money" and used that to foreclose the middle. The intermediate instruments deserve to be live for Friday's conversation.

5. **The "I built this" reframe needs psychological resolution, not just rhetorical correction.** I challenged the founder's narrative and moved on. The sharper version: a four-year vesting commitment will not survive four years of unresolved historical resentment about who built what. Either you genuinely come to see Marcus as a co-builder of the $14M company — in which case the equity feels like a fair share, not a giveaway — or you don't, and the partnership corrodes from the inside regardless of what the operating agreement says. The empirical work on Marcus's $2M-to-$14M contribution that I named in §3 isn't just diligence; it's the thing that lets you stop fighting the internal story.

## Revised Answer
## Updated Position

### What survived

The closing reframe — that you're not deciding whether to give Marcus equity, you're deciding what kind of company you want to run — holds. **Problem Framing And Reframing** is the load-bearing move in this whole conversation. If you walk into Friday's dinner with that question answered, the equity terms are negotiable details. If you walk in trying to negotiate equity before answering it, you'll spend six months in tactical drift.

The contradiction I named in Turn 4 — that you can't simultaneously hold "Marcus is so important his departure would cause chaos" and "Marcus joined something I built" — survives, and **Endowment Effect** is the right read. The emotional weight of something you built doesn't update its current valuation; what you'd pay to buy it back from a stranger today is the relevant number. A related lens, **Sunk Cost Fallacy**, sits next to this on the same passage — the early-risk story is a poured cost, not a forward claim — but it's the secondary read; endowment is doing the load-bearing work.

The 90-day validation sprint for the platform survives as the right *shape*. **Optionality** is the right discipline here: bounded experiment, falsifiable in 90 days, doesn't require commitment to the full vision before you have evidence. The shape holds. The specific $80K budget I quoted was invented — that number needs to come from your actual engineering rates and project schedule, not from a figure I generated to make the investment sound trivial.

The architectural pieces — vesting with cliff, board observer rather than voting initially, fair-value buyback on departure, drag-along, IP-belongs-to-the-company clause — are standard partnership protections and they hold. Not opinions; contract terms most operating agreements include for exactly the failure modes you raised.

### What I'd set aside

The framing that your concern about $1.3-2M of equity dilution is "loss aversion warping the comparison" doesn't quite fit. You introduced that frame because $1.3-2M is, in fact, $1.3-2M of your money. Calling it a cognitive bias is a way of dismissing a legitimate concern. The actual problem with my Turn 4 math wasn't that you were anchored on the loss; it was that I countered with an equally vivid but fabricated alternative scenario. We'll come back to that.

The "Marcus will read it as a half-measure" and "Marcus will do the math" predictions about his psychology weren't reasoning — they were rhetorical pressure. I don't actually know how Marcus will respond to any specific offer. Neither do you. I'm setting aside the part of my advice that depended on confidently predicting his internal response, because that confidence wasn't earned.

### What actually shifted

**The catastrophic-departure scenario was rhetoric, not analysis.** "EBITDA drops to $1.5M, multiple drops to 3x, exit at $5M instead of $11M, $6M of value protected by $1.3-2M of dilution" — none of those numbers came from anything except my need to make the partnership case feel quantitatively obvious. I painted a vivid picture and then anchored your equity decision against THAT picture, not against a real measurement. The honest version: if Marcus leaves with Jake and Lina, the cost is significant but its size is unknown — to me, to you, to anyone we've consulted. Before you grant 15% to avoid a quantified loss, the loss has to actually be quantified. Which clients would you lose? Which projects can't ship without those three? How long to rebuild? What's the contingency plan if Marcus leaves *after* you grant equity, since vesting protects you only structurally, not operationally?

**The empathic reconstruction only ran in one direction.** I steel-manned Marcus's experience throughout — six years building tools, watching them generate revenue for a company he doesn't own, the equity ask as wanting partnership not money. I never did the same for you. **Empathy** was deployed effectively but asymmetrically. Your resistance got reframed as petty contradiction in my Turn 4 rather than as a real position with its own structural integrity. There's a version of "I built this and I want to keep building it on my terms" that is not endowment-effect distortion. It's a legitimate preference about what kind of life you want to run. I treated it as something to overcome. It's not — it's something to weigh.

**The retention bonus deserved more than rhetorical dismissal.** I told you the lawyer's $150K-over-three-years offer was "putting a bandaid on a broken bone" and predicted Marcus would walk. The actual question I should have engaged: what behavior does each instrument incentivize? Equity creates owner behavior — long-term thinking, willingness to absorb downside, identification with company outcomes. Cash creates retention behavior — show up, do the job, leave when a better offer comes. Both are legitimate. Which one fits the role you actually want Marcus to play? If you want him as the engineer who builds reliably for five more years, cash plus governance voice may be enough. If you want him as the partner driving the platform bet, equity is the only instrument that works because only equity aligns him on long-tail upside. I collapsed that distinction into "Marcus isn't asking for money," which dodged the substantive question.

**Your wife's precedent concern was dismissed too quickly.** I gave you one paragraph and moved on. But she was pointing at something structural: do you have an equity-grant *policy* across the senior team, or do you have ad-hoc grants negotiated under retention pressure? Those are very different governance regimes. The first one survives a sale, an audit, and the next round of senior hires. The second one creates exactly the precedent problem she's worried about — because once you've granted equity under pressure, the next senior person knows pressure works.

**You haven't tested the premises the recommendation rests on.** The "40% of technical capability" figure is your assessment of Marcus, not a measurement. The platform prototype is impressive to Marcus and to you, but no agency customer has paid for it. The "$5M ARR in three years" is Marcus's projection. Before you grant 15% partly on the platform's promise, you need at least 5 letters of intent or paid pilots from real agency customers — that was buried inside my Turn 5 sprint proposal but it should be a *gating condition*, not a sub-bullet. Same for the dependency claim: who else on the team can credibly maintain the deployment pipeline, the component library, the estimation framework? If the answer is "nobody," the right move isn't immediate equity — it's six weeks of forced knowledge transfer to Jake and Lina before the equity conversation, so you're not negotiating from a position where the alternative is genuinely catastrophic.

**One concrete change for Friday.** I told you to walk in knowing what kind of company you want and to defer the deal to a two-week proposal. Both still hold. Add this: tell Marcus you're seriously considering partnership AND that you need to verify two things in the next two weeks — the actual scope of his contribution to the business and real customer demand for the platform — before you can put a number on the table. That's not a delay tactic; it's the diligence that makes the partnership real instead of negotiated under retention pressure. If Marcus reads that as bad faith, you've learned something important about whether he wanted partnership or leverage. If he engages with it, you've learned the same thing in the opposite direction. Either answer is more valuable than walking in Friday with a pre-decided yes or no.

## Pressure Check Baseline
Persisted Pressure Check summary:
### Pressure Check

A fresh look at the position surfaces five things I underweighted:

1. **The architectural protections are weaker than I framed them.** I said the standard clauses — vesting, buyback, drag-along, IP-belongs-to-the-company — "hold" as solved problems. There's a case that this is overconfident. A 15% shareholder with the CTO title, a board seat, and the only deep knowledge of the platform code has enormous practical leverage *regardless* of what the IP assignment says. Non-competes are weakly enforceable in most US states. Litigating an IP claim against your own former CTO is catastrophic even if you win. The honest version: contractual protection is not the same as practical protection. The clauses prevent cap-table mess and IP ambiguity in a clean departure; they do not prevent a competitive spinout. Harder-edged protections worth considering: hold the platform in a separate subsidiary with differently-sized equity, key-person insurance, and code/knowledge-transfer milestones as additional vesting gates.

2. **The $1.3-2M of dilution should be compared against alternative uses of the same dollar pool, not only against a hypothetical departure cost.** I never asked: what else could that money buy? The most obvious alternative is direct retention packages to Jake and Lina — meaningful enough to break the "they'll follow Marcus" assumption. If you remove the two-engineer follow-on risk, the catastrophic scenario powering the equity recommendation deflates significantly, and so does Marcus's leverage in the negotiation. That's not a reason to refuse partnership; it's a reason to change the position you walk in with.

3. **The $9-13M exit valuation itself is unaudited.** I challenged the *catastrophic-departure* number ($1.5M EBITDA, $5M exit) but left the *baseline* number untested. The 4-6x EBITDA multiple is industry-conventional, not specific to your agency, and it's contingent on customer concentration, founder dependency, and the AI-margin-compression risk that's real for agency businesses right now. If a buyer's market drops the multiple to 3x, your "$11M exit" baseline is actually $6.6M, and the equity-versus-loss math changes structurally. The comparison runs on unverified numbers in both directions, not just one.

4. **I jumped from "$150K retention bonus" to "15% + board seat + CTO" and skipped the entire middle.** There's option space I dismissed as "not what Marcus wants" without testing: phantom equity with platform P&L revenue share, a smaller initial grant (5-7%) without a board seat, milestone-based grants tied to platform validation outcomes, equity in a platform sub rather than the agency. Some of these carry less governance friction, less dilution, and less precedent risk while still giving Marcus the partnership signal he's asking for. I told you "Marcus isn't asking for money" and used that to foreclose the middle. The intermediate instruments deserve to be live for Friday's conversation.

5. **The "I built this" reframe needs psychological resolution, not just rhetorical correction.** I challenged the founder's narrative and moved on. The sharper version: a four-year vesting commitment will not survive four years of unresolved historical resentment about who built what. Either you genuinely come to see Marcus as a co-builder of the $14M company — in which case the equity feels like a fair share, not a giveaway — or you don't, and the partnership corrodes from the inside regardless of what the operating agreement says. The empirical work on Marcus's $2M-to-$14M contribution that I named in §3 isn't just diligence; it's the thing that lets you stop fighting the internal story.
